1: Psalm 65 is a hymn for the harvest — one of the great nature-and-worship psalms. לְךָ דֻמִיָּה תְהִלָּה (lĕkā dûmiyyāh tĕhillāh, "Praise awaits you in silence, O God, in Zion") — dûmiyyāh: silence (as in Psalms 62:1 — the expectant, laden silence). Tĕhillāh: praise (the root of Psalms — tĕhillîm). The phrase is paradoxical: "silence is praise" — the deepest worship is the awed silence before the divine presence that transcends words. The Talmudic interpretation (b. Megillah 18a): "silence is the best medicine for prayer" — the highest praise before Zion's God is the silence of awe. וְלְךָ יְשֻׁלַּם-נֶדֶר (wĕlĕkā yĕshullam-neder, "and to you the vow shall be fulfilled") — shālam (pual: to be completed, fulfilled): the vow is paid in full at Zion.
2: שֹׁמֵעַ תְּפִלָּה (shōmêaʿ tĕpillāh, "O you who hear prayer") — the divine epithet: the one whose defining characteristic is that he hears prayer. עָדֶיךָ כָּל-בָּשָׂר יָבֹאוּ (ʿādêkā kol-bāśār yābōʾû, "to you all flesh shall come") — kol-bāśār: all flesh — the universal scope (as in Joel 2:28/Acts 2:17: "I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh"). The prayer-hearing God draws all flesh — the entire mortal world — toward himself. The harvest psalm is also the universal-access psalm: the God who provides grain for all the earth also receives all the earth's prayer.
3: דִּבְרֵי עֲוֹנֹת גָּבְרוּ עָלַי (dibrê ʿăwōnōt gābĕrû ʿālay, "When our sins overwhelm us") — ʿāwōn: iniquity, perversity; gābar (qal: to be strong, to overpower): the sins become stronger than the Psalmist — they overwhelm and overcome. פְשָׁעֵינוּ אַתָּה תְכַפְּרֵם (pĕshāʿênû ʾattāh tĕkappĕrêm, "you atone for our transgressions") — peshaʿ: rebellious transgression (willful defiance — as in Psalms 32:1); kāpar (piel: to atone, to cover, to wipe clean — the verb of the Day of Atonement/Yôm hakkippûrîm). The atonement is entirely God's action: "you atone" — not "we atone." The divine covering of what the human cannot remove.
4: אַשְׁרֵי תִּבְחַר וּתְקָרֵב (ʾashrê tiḇḥar ûtĕqārêb, "Blessed is the one you choose and bring near") — the divine election (bāḥar: to choose — Deuteronomy 7:6; Isaiah 41:8-9) paired with access (qārab: to bring near — the priestly drawing-near of Leviticus 10:3). Election and access are both divine initiatives: God chooses and God draws near. יִשְׁכֹּן חֲצֵרֶיךָ (yishkōn ḥăṣêrêkā, "to dwell in your courts") — shākan: to dwell (the Mishkān/Shekinah root — the dwelling in God's courts as the experience of his dwelling-presence). נִשְׂבְּעָה בְּטוּב בֵּיתֶךָ (niśbĕʿāh bĕṭûb bêtekā, "we shall be satisfied with the goodness of your house") — śābaʿ: to be satisfied, to be full (as in Psalms 63:5 — the fat-and-marrow satisfaction). The goodness (ṭûb) of the divine house — all the goodness concentrated in the dwelling-place of God.
5: נוֹרָאוֹת בְּצֶדֶק תַּעֲנֵנוּ (nôrāʾôt bĕṣedeq taʿănênû, "By awesome deeds in righteousness you answer us") — nôrāʾôt: awesome acts (things that inspire holy fear — from yārēʾ: to fear); ṣedeq: righteousness (the acts are awesome and righteous simultaneously). אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׁעֵנוּ (ʾĕlōhê yishʿênû, "O God of our salvation") — the God whose defining character is salvation. מִבְטָח כָּל-קַצְוֵי-אֶרֶץ וְיָם רְחֹקִים (mibṭaḥ kol-qaṣwê-ʾereṣ wĕyām rĕḥōqîm, "you are the hope of all the ends of the earth and of the most distant seas") — mibṭāḥ: the ground of trust, the hope (from bāṭaḥ). The scope is universal: the ends of the earth and the farthest seas — the entire inhabited world has its ground of hope in this one God.
6: מֵכִין הָרִים בְּכֹחוֹ (mêkîn hārîm bĕkōḥô, "the one who by his strength sets the mountains in place") — kûn (hiphil: to establish, to set firm): the divine Creator-Establisher who fixed the mountains in their positions. Kōaḥ: strength, might (not the relational ḥesed but the raw power of creation). נֶאְזָר בִּגְבוּרָה (neʾzār bigĕbûrāh, "who is clothed with power") — ʾāzar (niphal: to be girded, to wear as a belt): power (gĕbûrāh) as the divine garment. The creator-God wearing strength as a warrior wears his belt — power not acquired but constitutive.
7: מַשְׁבִּיחַ שְׁאוֹן יַמִּים (mashbîaḥ shĕʾôn yammîm, "you still the roaring of the seas") — shābat (hiphil: to cause to cease — the Sabbath-rest root): God making the sea's roar cease (as in Psalms 46:9 — "he makes wars cease"). Shĕʾôn yammîm: the roar of the seas — the same cosmic chaos-force. שְׁאוֹן גַּלֵּיהֶם (shĕʾôn gallêhem, "the roaring of their waves") — the waves' tumult. וַהֲמוֹן לְאֻמִּים (wahămôn lĕʾummîm, "and the uproar of the nations") — the same verb (shābat) applied to the political world: the divine stilling of the sea's chaos and the nations' tumult are parallel acts of the same creative governance.
8: וַיִּירְאוּ יֹשְׁבֵי קְצָווֹת (wayyîrĕʾû yōshĕbê qĕṣāwôt, "Those who live at earth's farthest bounds stand in awe of your signs") — qĕṣāwôt: the ends/limits (as in Isaiah 41:9: "I took you from the ends of the earth"). The furthest peoples stand in awe (yārēʾ). מוֹצָאֵי-בֹקֶר וָעֶרֶב תַּרְנִין (môṣāʾê-bōqer wāʿereb tarnîn, "you make the sunrise and the sunset ring with joy") — môṣāʾê bōqer: "the exits/departures of morning" — the sunrise; ʿereb: evening. Rānan (hiphil: to cause to sing/ring with joy): God making the sunrise and sunset themselves ring with joyful song — the entire daily cycle is a divine shout of joy. Creation's own praise.
9: פָּקַדְתָּ הָאָרֶץ וַתְּשֹׁקְקֶהָ (pāqadtā hāʾāreṣ wattĕshōqĕqehā, "You visit the earth and water it abundantly") — pāqad: to visit, to attend to (the divine inspection of covenantal care — as God "visited" his people in Exodus 3:16; Psalms 4:31). Shāqaq (polel: to water abundantly, to drench): the divine farmer soaking his field with water. רַבַּת תַּעְשְׁרֶנָּה (rabbat taʿshĕrenāh, "you enrich it greatly") — ʿāshar (piel: to make rich, to enrich): the earth enriched by divine attention. פֶּלֶג אֱלֹהִים מָלֵא מָיִם (peleg ʾĕlōhîm mālêʾ māyim, "with the river of God, full of water") — peleg ʾĕlōhîm: "the river of God" — the heavenly water-supply (as in Job 38:25-28: the channels of water that God directs). The rain is the river of God arriving from above.
10: תְּלָמֶיהָ רַוֵּה נַחֵת גְּדוּדֶיהָ (tĕlāmêhā rāwwêh naḥêt gĕdûdêhā, "you soak its furrows abundantly, you level its ridges") — rāwāh (piel: to saturate, to drench — as the drenched man is fully soaked): the furrows flooded with water. Nāḥat (hiphil: to bring down, to level): flattening the high ridges between furrows. בִּרְבִיבִים תְּמֹגְגֶנָּה (birbîbîm tĕmōgĕgenāh, "you soften it with showers") — rĕbîbîm: abundant showers (the spring rains — Deuteronomy 32:2: "let my teaching drop as the rain, my speech distill as the dew, like gentle rain upon tender grass, and like showers upon the herb"). Māwag (polel: to soften, to dissolve): rain making the hard ground soft for planting.
11: עִטַּרְתָּ שְׁנַת טוֹבָתֶךָ (ʿiṭṭartā shĕnat ṭôbātekā, "You crown the year with your bounty") — ʿāṭar (piel: to encircle with a crown — to put a crown on): the year itself crowned. Ṭôbāh: goodness, bounty. The entire agricultural year, from spring planting to autumn harvest, is treated as if it were a king receiving a crown of goodness — the year of harvest is God's royal gift. וּמַעְגָּלֶיךָ יִרְעֲפוּן דָּשֶׁן (ûmaʿgālêkā yirʿăpûn dāshen, "your wagon tracks overflow with abundance") — maʿgāl: a track, a wheel-rut, a path (the tracks of God's divine chariot-wagon that has traveled over the earth bringing the harvest). Rāʿap: to drop, to overflow (the wagon-tracks of the divine harvester overflowing with the fat of the harvest). God moving through the earth as a farmer moves through his field.
12: יִרְעֲפוּ נְאוֹת מִדְבָּר (yirʿăpû nĕʾôt midbar, "the pastures of the wilderness overflow") — even the midbar (wilderness/steppes — the place without permanent water) receives the overflow: the abundance spills from the cultivated fields into the wild pastures. וְגִיל גְּבָעוֹת תַּחְגֹּרְנָה (wĕgîl gĕbāʿôt taḥgōrĕnāh, "the hills are wrapped in rejoicing") — gîl: the dancing-joy (the physical exultation of movement — as in Psalms 45:15); ḥāgar: to gird, to wrap around. The hills girdled/clothed with rejoicing — the landscape dressed in celebration. The inanimate hills personified as joyful dancers girding themselves for the harvest-feast.
13: לָבְשׁוּ כָרִים הַצֹּאן (lābĕshû kārîm haṣṣōʾn, "the meadows are dressed with flocks") — lābash: to be clothed — the meadows wear the flocks as their garment. וַעֲמָקִים יַעַטְפוּ-בָר (waʿămāqîm yaʿaṭpû-bār, "the valleys are blanketed with grain") — ʿāṭap: to cover, to envelop (as a cloak envelops — as in Psalms 73:6: pride as a necklace/chain); bār: grain, pure grain (the produce of the threshing floor). יִתְרוֹעֲעוּ אַף-יָשִׁירוּ (yitrôʿăʿû ʾap-yāshîrû, "they shout for joy, they break into song!") — the personified landscape in full-throated praise: the shout (rûaʿ: the battle-cry/acclamation of joy) and the song (shîr). The creation itself joins the psalm of praise — the harvest the occasion for universal natural praise that closes the Psalm with "all the earth" joining the worship begun at Zion (v1).
